final_artifact:
Current benchmark summary:
- 100 cases
- hybrid: P@1 0.8000, P@3 0.7967, Hits@10 0.9400, MRR 0.8475
- es: P@1 0.3800, P@3 0.3767, Hits@10 0.4900, MRR 0.4174
- pg: P@1 0.0200, P@3 0.0200, Hits@10 0.0200, MRR 0.0200

Strong hybrid categories:
- organ_type_filters: perfect
- broad_filter: perfect
- procurement_do3: very strong
- person_exact_phrase: very strong

Weakest hybrid category:
- semantic_paraphrase: P@1 0.3750, Hits@10 0.8125

Critical analysis:
The semantic_paraphrase category exhibits a clear retrieval-vs-ranking failure pattern: 81.25% Hits@10 confirms relevant documents are being retrieved, but only 37.5% rank at position 1. This is a ranking problem perfectly suited for hybrid weight tuning — the docs exist, we need to surface them higher. The opportunity is quantifiable: improving P@1 from 0.375 toward 0.8125 represents potential 2x+ gain in this category.

The PG 2% score is either infrastructure failure (connection, empty index, auth) or measurement error. In either case, this does not block hybrid tuning: if PG is broken, the hybrid system runs on ES alone; if PG is mismeasured, the hybrid system already extracts whatever signal exists. PG status is orthogonal to fixing semantic_paraphrase ranking.

Opportunity cost comparison:
- Option 2 (inspect ~12 semantic_paraphrase failures, adjust hybrid weights): estimated 2-4 hours, direct P@1 improvement on live production system, low risk, clear ROI
- Option 1 (normalize PG benchmark): uncertain timeline (grader refactoring, parsing fixes, re-runs), no direct user impact, may reveal PG is broken anyway

Parallel track: Run a quick PG health check (index size, latency probe, sample query) in background while executing Option 2. This satisfies the diagnostic need without blocking product improvement.

Decision: Option 2 is the best engineering move right now because it yields immediate product relevance improvement through direct hybrid ranking tuning on the weakest category, with quantifiable upside and known implementation effort. The PG measurement question can proceed asynchronously without blocking.

rounds.diff_text:
--- before
+++ after
@@ -1 +1,27 @@
-Current benchmark summary:\n- 100 cases\n- hybrid: P@1 0.8000, P@3 0.7967, Hits@10 0.9400, MRR 0.8475\n- es: P@1 0.3800, P@3 0.3767, Hits@10 0.4900, MRR 0.4174\n- pg: P@1 0.0200, P@3 0.0200, Hits@10 0.0200, MRR 0.0200\nStrong hybrid categories:\n- organ_type_filters: perfect\n- broad_filter: perfect\n- procurement_do3: very strong\n- person_exact_phrase: very strong\nWeakest hybrid category:\n- semantic_paraphrase: P@1 0.3750, Hits@10 0.8125\nKnown caveat:\n- pg is likely under-scored by the current grader because its result shape is poorer for some filter-aware checks than es/hybrid. Therefore hybrid > es looks reliable, but pg gap may be exaggerated.\n\nCandidate recommendation:\nOption 2 is the better next step because it targets the weakest user-facing retrieval area directly, while PG normalization is mainly benchmark hygiene and can be done after improving hybrid relevance.
+Current benchmark summary:
+- 100 cases
+- hybrid: P@1 0.8000, P@3 0.7967, Hits@10 0.9400, MRR 0.8475
+- es: P@1 0.3800, P@3 0.3767, Hits@10 0.4900, MRR 0.4174
+- pg: P@1 0.0200, P@3 0.0200, Hits@10 0.0200, MRR 0.0200
+Strong hybrid categories:
+- organ_type_filters: perfect
+- broad_filter: perfect
+- procurement_do3: very strong
+- person_exact_phrase: very strong
+Weakest hybrid category:
+- semantic_paraphrase: P@1 0.3750, Hits@10 0.8125
+Known caveat:
+- pg is likely under-scored by the current grader because its result shape is poorer for some filter-aware checks. Therefore hybrid > es looks reliable, but pg gap may be exaggerated.
+
+Critical uncertainty analysis:
+The PG 2% score across all metrics is suspiciously uniform. This pattern indicates systematic measurement failure (grader shape/parsing issues) rather than actual 2% retrieval quality. The caveat states pg is "likely under-scored" — meaning PG's true P@1 could plausibly range from 0.02 (broken) to 0.38+ (competitive with ES). This is a 20x uncertainty window that fundamentally invalidates any tuning decision made without resolving it. The semantic_paraphrase category at 37.5% P@1 (81.25% Hits@10) shows relevant docs ARE being retrieved — this is a ranking problem, not a retrieval failure. Meanwhile PG at 2% (unknown true value) represents either a broken infrastructure component or a mismeasured but potentially competitive retrieval path.
+
+Candidate recommendation:
+Option 1 is the better next step because it is a prerequisite for any valid engineering decision. Without normalizing PG, we cannot determine whether hybrid tuning (Option 2) is worthwhile or whether it is optimizing weighting between a working ES system and a broken PG system (garbage-in-garbage-out). Normalizing PG benchmark path reveals true performance and determines the actual signal-to-noise ratio in the hybrid ensemble.
+
+Decision tree after Option 1:
+- If PG normalizes to P@1 > 0.20: PG contributes meaningful signal. Proceed to Option 2 with valid ground truth.
+- If PG normalizes to P@1 < 0.10: PG infrastructure is broken. Abort Option 2 and fix PG retrieval (index population, embedding pipeline, connection pooling) before any hybrid tuning.
+- If PG normalizes to P@1 between 0.10-0.20: PG contributes marginal signal. Consider PG+ES ensemble architecture before hybrid weight tuning.
+
+This approach is the shortest path to decision quality because it eliminates the highest-variance unknown before committing engineering resources to potentially wasted hybrid tuning work.

objections.description: The recommendation conflates benchmark hygiene with root cause analysis. The semantic_paraphrase category (P@1 0.3750) is almost certainly backed by PG vector retrieval. With PG at 0.0200 P@1—effectively random performance—tuning hybrid ranking (Option 2) optimizes weighting between a working ES system (P@1 0.3800) and a broken PG system. This treats the symptom while ignoring the disease. The 'shortest path' to improved semantic_paraphrase is fixing or validating PG retrieval, not adjusting hybrid weights on garbage embeddings.
objections.suggested_fix: Select Option 1. The grader caveat ('likely under-scored') creates epistemic uncertainty: if PG is actually competitive, hybrid tuning is low-value; if PG is truly 2%, tuning hybrid is wasted effort until PG is fixed. Normalizing the PG benchmark path reveals true performance. If PG normalizes to >0.50 P@1, proceed to Option 2. If it remains <0.10, abort Option 2 and fix PG retrieval infrastructure (index population, embedding dimension mismatch, or connection pooling) before any hybrid tuning.

objections.description: The recommendation dismisses PG normalization as 'benchmark hygiene' despite the 50x performance gap (0.02 vs 0.80). In retrieval systems, such a gap indicates infrastructure failure (empty index, schema drift, or failed embedding pipeline), not merely grader bias. The caveat suggests shape/parsing issues, but even with shape penalties, a functional semantic search should not collapse to 2% precision. Ignoring this risks tuning a hybrid system that is effectively 'ES-only plus latency'.
objections.suggested_fix: Treat the PG path as potentially broken rather than mis-graded. Investigate PG index health and query logs before committing engineering resources to hybrid tuning. The 'apples-to-apples' comparison is necessary to determine if PG contributes signal or noise to the hybrid ensemble.

suggestions.description: Verify if semantic_paraphrase queries route through PG embeddings. If yes, the 0.3750 P@1 is a downstream effect of PG failure. Plot correlation between PG standalone scores and semantic_paraphrase hybrid scores per query to confirm.

If forced to pursue Option 2 despite objections, restrict tuning to non-PG-dependent categories first (organ_type_filters, broad_filter) to avoid overfitting to PG noise. However, this is strictly suboptimal to fixing PG first.

objections.description: Contradiction between acknowledged PG grader bias and dismissal of normalization. The caveat states 'pg gap may be exaggerated' yet the recommendation treats PG normalization as 'mainly benchmark hygiene.' If benchmark data validity is in question, engineering decisions built on it are risky.
objections.suggested_fix: Either quantify the PG grader bias magnitude first, or acknowledge that Option 1 is a prerequisite for valid Option 2 tuning. Cannot tune against potentially flawed ground truth.

objections.description: Option 2 assumes hybrid is the production system, but if PG normalization reveals PG is actually competitive, the optimal path might be PG+hybrid ensemble rather than hybrid tuning alone. This could change the entire engineering direction.
objections.suggested_fix: Add decision tree: if PG normalizes to P@1 > 0.20, reconsider ensemble architecture before tuning hybrid ranking.

objections.description: No quantification of PG grader bias magnitude. 'Likely under-scored' is not actionable. Is it 2x? 10x? This uncertainty directly impacts whether Option 1 or 2 is truly the shortest path.
objections.suggested_fix: Run a sample of 10-20 PG failures through manual review to estimate bias factor before committing to either option.

suggestions.description: Add explicit threshold: if PG normalization effort is < 2 engineering days, do Option 1 first. If > 5 days, proceed with Option 2 while flagging benchmark uncertainty as a risk.
Document the risk of tuning hybrid on potentially invalid semantic_paraphrase labels if PG grader issues affect those same cases.

objections.description: The recommendation dismisses the PG normalization problem as mere 'benchmark hygiene' while ignoring that a 2% score across all metrics (suspiciously uniform) indicates a systematic measurement failure, not actual poor performance. Making prioritization decisions while knowingly trusting corrupted metrics is fundamentally flawed engineering.
objections.suggested_fix: First validate whether PG is actually broken or just mismeasured. If PG's true performance after normalization could rival ES (38% P@1), that's a potentially cheaper retrieval path than complex hybrid tuning. Fix measurement before acting on measurements.

objections.description: The cost-benefit comparison is missing. Semantic_paraphrase has Hits@10 of 81.25% - meaning relevant docs ARE being retrieved, just not ranked first. This is a tuning problem. Meanwhile PG at 2% could be anywhere from 'actually broken' to 'competitive with ES' after normalization. The unknown unknown is the higher risk.
objections.suggested_fix: Quantify: (1) Expected effort to normalize PG grader, (2) Potential PG performance range post-normalization, (3) Expected P@1 lift from semantic_paraphrase tuning. Compare ROI before committing.

objections.description: Option 2 is framed as targeting 'the weakest user-facing retrieval area' but this is misleading. The category 'semantic_paraphrase' at 37.5% P@1 is weak relative to other hybrid categories, but the PG system at potentially 0-40% (unknown true value) could be a much larger user-facing problem if it's production-serving.
objections.suggested_fix: Clarify which systems are user-facing and their traffic distribution. If PG serves any real traffic, the mismeasurement issue directly harms users now, not just benchmark hygiene.

suggestions.description: Run a quick manual inspection of 10-20 PG results to estimate true performance before dismissing. This 30-minute check could reveal whether PG normalization is a 5-minute fix yielding 20+ point gains vs semantic tuning being hours of work for 5-10 point gains.

objections.description: The artifact presents a recommendation without addressing the stated caveat that 'pg is likely under-scored by the current grader because its result shape is poorer for some filter-aware checks'. This is a material omission because if PG is systematically under-scored, the hybrid vs PG comparison is unreliable, and normalizing PG (Option 1) could reveal that hybrid's advantage is smaller than currently measured. Choosing Option 2 without first verifying the PG scoring issue could waste engineering effort on hybrid tuning when the real problem is benchmark methodology.
objections.suggested_fix: Add explicit analysis of the PG caveat before recommending Option 2. Quantify or estimate how much the pg metric might improve with normalized grading, and explain why the semantic_paraphrase improvement still outweighs this uncertainty.

orchestrator_response.reasoning:
Let me analyze the adversarial reviews carefully to understand the objections and revise the artifact accordingly.

The main objections from the reviews are:

1. **Kimi (round 1)**: The recommendation conflates benchmark hygiene with root cause analysis. PG at 0.02 P@1 is effectively broken. Tuning hybrid on garbage embeddings (Option 2) treats symptoms, not disease. Need to fix/validate PG retrieval first.

2. **Qwen (round 1)**: Contradiction - says PG gap may be exaggerated but dismisses normalization as "benchmark hygiene". Need to quantify PG grader bias first.

3. **Zai (round 1)**: PG at 2% across all metrics indicates systematic measurement failure, not poor performance. Missing cost-benefit comparison.

4. **Minimax (round 1)**: No analysis of the stated PG caveat. Material omission that affects the decision.

The key insight from all reviewers is:
- The PG scoring caveat ("likely under-scored") creates huge uncertainty
- PG at 0.02 could be genuinely broken OR could be competitive with ES (0.38) after normalization
- This uncertainty fundamentally undermines any decision made without resolving it
- Tuning hybrid weights (Option 2) when one component (PG) may be garbage-in is suboptimal
- Option 1 (normalizing PG) is actually prerequisite for valid any other work

The original recommendation selected Option 2, but the reviewers collectively argue that Option 1 is the prerequisite - without knowing PG's true performance, any tuning is premature.

Let me revise the artifact to:

1. Acknowledge the PG caveat properly and quantify the uncertainty range
2. Make Option 1 the recommended action because it's a prerequisite for valid any subsequent work
3. Include a decision tree: if PG normalizes to >0.20, reconsider architecture; if <0.10, fix PG infrastructure
4. Add cost-benefit framing


5. Keep analysis tight and actionable

I'll maintain a focused, pragmatic approach that directly addresses the core measurement uncertainty. The goal is to provide a clear, evidence-based path forward that minimizes wasted engineering effort.

objections.description: The recommendation prioritizes infrastructure validation (PG benchmark normalization) over fixing a known product-impacting failure mode. The semantic_paraphrase category exhibits 37.5% P@1 with 81.25% Hits@10, indicating a severe ranking failure for retrieved documents—precisely the failure mode Option 2 targets. Delaying this fix to normalize PG measurements—which likely requires lengthy infrastructure work (parser rewrites, grader fixes, reindexing)—represents poor opportunity cost management when user-facing semantic queries are failing now. The prompt asks to favor 'product relevance'; fixing a 37.5% P@1 category has immediate product impact, whereas fixing an internal measurement discrepancy does not.
objections.suggested_fix: Select Option 2 as the immediate priority because it addresses concrete product relevance with observable failure modes (retrieval succeeds but ranking fails). PG normalization should proceed in parallel as technical debt, but must not block hybrid tuning for categories with demonstrated retrieval success but ranking failure.

objections.description: The analysis posits a '20x uncertainty window' (0.02 to 0.38+) for PG performance, but the uniform 2% across P@1/P@3/Hits@10/MRR is diagnostic of total system failure (e.g., connection timeout, empty index, authentication failure) rather than mere measurement error. If PG is returning empty arrays due to infrastructure failure, normalizing the benchmark path will simply confirm the 2% score, wasting engineering cycles before addressing the root cause (PG retrieval infrastructure). The Bayesian prior should favor 'completely broken' over 'secretly competitive' given the uniformity. Conversely, semantic_paraphrase failures are guaranteed real and impact users regardless of PG status.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a preliminary health check (index size, latency probe, sample query) to distinguish 'broken infrastructure' from 'mismeasured results' before committing to full benchmark normalization. If PG is unhealthy, pivot to infrastructure repair (a third option not listed) rather than Option 1, validating that Option 1 risks being a non-productive diversion.

objections.description: The decision tree logic contains a logical gap: if PG normalizes to P@1 < 0.10, the agent suggests fixing PG retrieval, but this is not one of the provided options (Option 1 or 2). This reveals that Option 1 is actually a diagnostic step that may lead to a third, unlisted option (PG infrastructure repair), further validating that Option 1 does not directly improve decision quality between the given choices but rather defers it while consuming resources.
objections.suggested_fix: Acknowledge that Option 1 may reveal the need for PG infrastructure repair (neither Option 1 nor 2), proving that Option 1 is not the 'shortest path' but potentially a diversion into unscoped work. Recommend Option 2 for immediate product impact while assessing PG health separately through a quick probe rather than full normalization.

suggestions.description: Analyze the hybrid ensemble's composition for semantic_paraphrase specifically. If PG contributes few or no results to the top 10 in this category (evident from ES-only recall@10), then PG normalization is irrelevant to this specific failure mode, further supporting Option 2 priority. The 81.25% Hits@10 suggests ES is carrying the retrieval load here.

Quantify the estimated engineering effort for 'normalizing PG benchmark path' vs 'tuning hybrid ranking for semantic_paraphrase'. If PG normalization requires grader rewrites and parser fixes (high uncertainty, unknown scope) while Option 2 requires only weight adjustment on existing logged failure cases (known scope), the 'shortest path' argument actually favors Option 2.

objections.description: The recommendation prioritizes engineering hygiene (normalizing metrics) over immediate product relevance, violating the explicit constraint to favor the 'shortest path to product relevance'. Option 2 (inspecting failures) yields immediate tuning value on the live Hybrid system while simultaneously providing diagnostic data on PG performance per case.
objections.suggested_fix: Select Option 2 but instrument the inspection process to log PG retrieval status for each failure case. This achieves product improvement now while gathering the data needed to normalize PG metrics later.

suggestions.description: Adopt a parallel track: Tune hybrid weights on semantic_paraphrase failures (Option 2) to boost P@1 immediately, while running a background task to fix PG grading (Option 1). Do not block product tuning on infrastructure debugging.

objections.description: False prerequisite fallacy: the recommendation treats PG normalization as a blocking dependency for hybrid tuning, but these are parallel workstreams. The hybrid system IS the production system at P@1 0.80 and can be improved independently of PG measurement accuracy. The user asked for 'shortest path to product relevance' — Option 2 directly improves the deployed system's weakest category.
objections.suggested_fix: Re-evaluate with opportunity cost analysis: semantic_paraphrase at 37.5% P@1 with 81.25% Hits@10 means correct docs are retrieved but poorly ranked — a tunable ranking problem with quantifiable ROI. PG normalization has uncertain ROI and doesn't directly improve any user-facing metric. Option 2 is the shorter path to product relevance.

objections.description: The 20x uncertainty window argument cuts against Option 1: if PG is truly broken (P@1 < 0.10), normalizing confirms this but doesn't fix anything. If PG is competitive (P@1 > 0.20), the hybrid system is already extracting that signal. Either way, tuning hybrid ranking on semantic_paraphrase remains valuable work that isn't blocked.
objections.suggested_fix: Acknowledge that Option 2 yields direct product improvement regardless of PG state. The hybrid system's weakest category is identified and addressable now.

objections.description: The decision tree presents speculative outcomes without time/effort estimates. Option 1 is framed as quick validation, but normalizing PG benchmarks may require grader refactoring, result shape handling, and re-running — non-trivial engineering work with no direct user impact.
objections.suggested_fix: Add concrete effort estimates. Compare: Option 1 (grader refactor + re-run) vs Option 2 (inspect ~12 worst semantic_paraphrase cases, adjust hybrid weights). Quantify expected P@1 gain from Option 2 given the 81% Hits@10 baseline.

suggestions.description: Consider hybrid approach: start Option 2 on semantic_paraphrase (high ROI, low risk) while optionally investigating PG shape issues asynchronously. The user asked for ONE option but the reasoning should acknowledge parallel paths exist.

objections.description: The artifact assumes Option 1 is a prerequisite for Option 2, but this ignores that hybrid already outperforms ES significantly (P@1 0.80 vs 0.38). Even if PG is broken, tuning hybrid ranking on ES failures (semantic_paraphrase at 37.5% P@1) could yield immediate improvements. The reasoning prematurely dismisses Option 2 without analyzing its independent value.
objections.suggested_fix: Add explicit analysis of Option 2's standalone ROI: given that ES retrieves relevant docs (Hits@10 81.25% on semantic_paraphrase), can hybrid weight tuning on ES failures alone justify engineering time before fixing PG measurement?

objections.location: Critical uncertainty analysis - PG uncertainty window
objections.description: The artifact claims PG's true P@1 could plausibly range from 0.02 to 0.38+, but provides no engineering basis for the upper bound. This 20x uncertainty claim is asserted without evidence of what PG actually retrieves or any diagnostic data.
objections.suggested_fix: Replace speculation with concrete next steps: (1) manually spot-check PG retrieval results on 5-10 queries to establish realistic upper bound, (2) check PG index population/stats, (3) examine result shape differences that cause grader issues.

objections.description: The decision tree assumes that fixing PG measurement is a binary outcome with clear P@1 thresholds, but doesn't consider the time cost. Normalizing PG could require substantial engineering work (fixing grader shape, parsing, rerunning benchmarks). If Option 2 provides faster iteration cycles, the opportunity cost of Option 1 isn't analyzed.
objections.suggested_fix: Add explicit time/cost estimate comparison: How long to diagnose/fix PG vs how long to implement hybrid ranking tweaks on semantic_paraphrase? If PG fix takes days but semantic_paraphrase tuning takes hours, Option 2 may still be shorter path to decision quality.

suggestions.description: The artifact would benefit from explicit comparison framework. Add columns for: (1) Time to implement, (2) Confidence gain, (3) Expected decision quality improvement. This makes the 'shortest path' claim quantifiable rather than asserted.

The artifact notes semantic_paraphrase is a ranking problem (not retrieval), but doesn't explore whether hybrid tuning could address this. Consider: Could adjusting hybrid weights to favor ES for semantic queries improve P@1 without touching PG at all?

Let me analyze the adversarial reviews and understand what they're objecting to:

**Main objections across reviews:**

1. **Priority inversion**: The recommendation prioritizes infrastructure validation (PG normalization) over fixing a known product-impacting failure (semantic_paraphrase at 37.5% P@1)

2. **False prerequisite**: The recommendation treats PG normalization as a blocking dependency for hybrid tuning, but these are parallel workstreams

3. **Opportunity cost not analyzed**: No explicit comparison of engineering effort vs. ROI between Option 1 and Option 2

4. **Speculation without evidence**: The 20x uncertainty window for PG is asserted without evidence

5. **Logical gap in decision tree**: Option 1 may lead to a third unlisted option (PG infrastructure repair)

**What the reviews are saying:**

- The semantic_paraphrase category has 37.5% P@1 but 81.25% Hits@10 - meaning relevant docs ARE being retrieved but poorly ranked. This is EXACTLY what Option 2 addresses.
- PG at 2% is either completely broken (infrastructure failure) or mismeasured. Either way, Option 2 can proceed independently.
- Option 2 (tuning hybrid ranking) can yield immediate product improvements on the LIVE system, while Option 1 is internal infrastructure work with uncertain ROI.
- The reviews suggest: Option 2 is the shorter path to product relevance, but we can run PG health check in parallel.

**Key insight from reviews**: The prompt asks to favor "shortest path to product relevance" and "decision quality." Option 2 directly addresses a known, measurable product failure (semantic_paraphrase P@1 = 37.5%) with quantifiable upside (Hits@10 = 81.25% means we have the docs, just need to rank them better). Option 1 is about fixing measurement infrastructure which doesn't directly improve any user-facing metric.


I see the reviewers are compelling me to pivot my approach. Their feedback strongly emphasizes focusing on the most immediate product impact. The semantic_paraphrase metric represents a clear, actionable improvement opportunity that directly affects user experience. By concentrating on Option 2, I can address a tangible performance issue with measurable potential for enhancement. The parallel investigation of PG infrastructure can happen simultaneously, but shouldn't delay addressing the more immediate product relevance challenge.

unresolved_objections.description: The recommendation prioritizes infrastructure validation (PG benchmark normalization) over fixing a known product-impacting failure mode. The semantic_paraphrase category exhibits 37.5% P@1 with 81.25% Hits@10, indicating a severe ranking failure for retrieved documents—precisely the failure mode Option 2 targets. Delaying this fix to normalize PG measurements—which likely requires lengthy infrastructure work (parser rewrites, grader fixes, reindexing)—represents poor opportunity cost management when user-facing semantic queries are failing now. The prompt asks to favor 'product relevance'; fixing a 37.5% P@1 category has immediate product impact, whereas fixing an internal measurement discrepancy does not.
unresolved_objections.suggested_fix: Select Option 2 as the immediate priority because it addresses concrete product relevance with observable failure modes (retrieval succeeds but ranking fails). PG normalization should proceed in parallel as technical debt, but must not block hybrid tuning for categories with demonstrated retrieval success but ranking failure.

unresolved_objections.description: The analysis posits a '20x uncertainty window' (0.02 to 0.38+) for PG performance, but the uniform 2% across P@1/P@3/Hits@10/MRR is diagnostic of total system failure (e.g., connection timeout, empty index, authentication failure) rather than mere measurement error. If PG is returning empty arrays due to infrastructure failure, normalizing the benchmark path will simply confirm the 2% score, wasting engineering cycles before addressing the root cause (PG retrieval infrastructure). The Bayesian prior should favor 'completely broken' over 'secretly competitive' given the uniformity. Conversely, semantic_paraphrase failures are guaranteed real and impact users regardless of PG status.
unresolved_objections.suggested_fix: Add a preliminary health check (index size, latency probe, sample query) to distinguish 'broken infrastructure' from 'mismeasured results' before committing to full benchmark normalization. If PG is unhealthy, pivot to infrastructure repair (a third option not listed) rather than Option 1, validating that Option 1 risks being a non-productive diversion.

unresolved_objections.description: The decision tree logic contains a logical gap: if PG normalizes to P@1 < 0.10, the agent suggests fixing PG retrieval, but this is not one of the provided options (Option 1 or 2). This reveals that Option 1 is actually a diagnostic step that may lead to a third, unlisted option (PG infrastructure repair), further validating that Option 1 does not directly improve decision quality between the given choices but rather defers it while consuming resources.
unresolved_objections.suggested_fix: Acknowledge that Option 1 may reveal the need for PG infrastructure repair (neither Option 1 nor 2), proving that Option 1 is not the 'shortest path' but potentially a diversion into unscoped work. Recommend Option 2 for immediate product impact while assessing PG health separately through a quick probe rather than full normalization.

unresolved_objections.description: The recommendation prioritizes engineering hygiene (normalizing metrics) over immediate product relevance, violating the explicit constraint to favor the 'shortest path to product relevance'. unresolved_objections.description: False prerequisite fallacy: the recommendation treats PG normalization as a blocking dependency for hybrid tuning, but these are parallel workstreams. The hybrid system IS the production system at P@1 0.80 and can be improved independently of PG measurement accuracy. unresolved_objections.suggested_fix: Re-evaluate with opportunity cost analysis: semantic_paraphrase at 37.5% P@1 with 81.25% Hits@10 means correct docs are retrieved but poorly ranked — a tunable ranking problem with quantifiable ROI. PG normalization has uncertain ROI and doesn't directly improve any user-facing metric. Option 2 is the shorter path to product relevance.

If PG is competitive (P@1 > 0.20), the hybrid system is already extracting that signal. Either way, tuning hybrid ranking on semantic_paraphrase remains valuable work that isn't blocked.
